item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like a random collection of things. If there was a connection or theme, I didn't understand it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's already an article in a previous Series that "doesn't have a title", so that aspect of this may not work, but for a skip about random stuff happening with occasional memes? Not bad. Worth an updoot. Doubly so when that earlier comment is taken into account.

Footnote 3 has an oops.

I am here for explosive chungus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not great, but certainly not worthy of a downvote. I wish it went into more detail of what exactly was happening: but like this it's just a bit… boring.
(Edit: the Chungus reference was also really jarring.)

Novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that's part of the point. There is a comment above that is the best explanation I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"big chungus"

just beautiful

item.title: missing links
item.content:encoded: hey I think this page is missing the links to 4338 and 4340

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get that — this is all highly subjective. Where one man sees noise, another sees mystery. One man's arrow-punctured horse is another man's unicorn. Tickling our "pareidolia gland" just enough to make us want to fill in the blanks is a complicated art (one which I am somewhat obsessed with).

I don't think this article succeeds in convincing the reader that there's anything of interest going on — for me, this was just a sequence of random events. But I acknowledge that when I say that, I'm really just saying it didn't succeed in convincing me that anything was going on. Certainly, if it worked for you — that's valid, and relevant to how I interpret the article.

I appreciate you elaborating on why you liked both articles. It's informative — particularly given my obsession with figuring out ways to accomplish the precise effect you're talking about!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After someone linked this one from the comment section of SCP-4564

Hey, that was me. Let me try to explain why I like these two articles so much:

+ Moderately long explanation
- Moderately long explanation

The first time I read this, I slowly made my way through it. Trying to figure out some kind of meaning or pattern to the entries was a lot of fun: there are a few elements that recur only once or twice, and it feels like the phrases all occupy the same… space in the media they were lifted from. I'm still not sure what the article means, though.

It almost seems like this SCP is confusing by design: the recurring elements seem to be spaced apart in such a way that someone carefully reading through the addendum would keep stumbling upon new red herrings. On top of that, the entries seem to emulate a rudimentary storyline without the use of real story elements: it begins with some confusing exposition, continues by referencing pop culture a bunch, suddenly becomes meta and then ends.

Not that I understand how or if it all connects, but it definitely isn't random. In fact, I imagine that an article that consisted of (somehow) completely random elements would be less effective than this. I don't believe the author was even trying to be random, since almost all of the phrases recited via -B are from very well-known pieces of media. The Big Chungus inclusion almost feels like it's trying to remind the reader that this was written by a person, as if they admit that there's no deeper meaning to it and that there's no use in worrying about it anymore.

I enjoyed this article a lot because I kept trying to make sense of it while it kept leading me in different directions as I got further into it. The climax and sudden end turned my attempts at understanding the SCP into a story in and of itself, with rising/falling action and all. It's like a well-designed puzzle, even though I didn't get to solve it. The probably soon-to-be deleted SCP-4564 did the exact same thing for me.

And maybe the author was in fact trying to be random, or perhaps they did hide some deeper meaning under several layers of abstraction. Regardless of whether I completely missed the point or not, I still got a lot of enjoyment out of reading this the way I did. The same goes for the current version of SCP-4564. They both put the internal logic just out of reach, and let the reader chase it for a while. It's like a treasure hunt where the treasure's inside of you all along.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After someone linked this one from the comment section of SCP-4564 (note to future readers; the linked SCP is in the negatives, and the comment section might be gone/different by the time you're seeing this post), I glanced at this one, and… I've got the same issue here as I did there, I think.

On one hand, there's something to be said for SCPs that are ultimately inscrutable; you can establish a powerful atmosphere by exploring the notion that SCPs are not things that tell a story, but genuine anomalies — things that are incomprehensible and strange.

But on the other hand… establishing that as a motif requires a feather-light touch. The moment you start talking about funny objects materializing in mid-air while referencing internet memes, the atmosphere vanishes in a poof of smoke — you've pulled back the contrivance and revealed the man behind the curtain.

I'm not certain what you were trying to achieve here — a lot of the moves struck me as interesting — but they also struck me as just being a random sequence of events picked by an author. And, I mean — that's clearly what they are!

But I think that the trick here is to convince me that they aren't.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Staff Post - Info
item.content:encoded: Purplayoss, you cannot title an SCP that you did not write. If the author chose to not include an out of universe title, that is their creative decision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still needs a title after nearly eight months. Is it okay if I add a title?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess I’m missing something, because this just seems like a lot of random stuff with no underlying theme or purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, i think I'll novote until a page title comes up, and if it clicks for me at that point, then I'll likely upvote! Unless NatVoltic's theory is correct, then you definitely won't give it a title and I fuckin applaud you for that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you think about this one, it begins to make more sense. I quite like SCP's like this one, where you have to think to understand. But it does have some areas that aren't so good. I'll give it an upvote.

There are scps which I understand reading once, there are scps which I understand reading twice, there are scps which I understand reading carefully, there are scps which I don't understand but I still like them, there are scps which I understand after a declass.

There are bland scps and I don't like them, and combined with scps which I don't understand this is the extremely rare case.

A heavy -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting that this still doesn't have a name on the mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read this three times over and still got nothing.

It's going to be a rare novote from me, as I can't tell if this is a perfectly-assembled logic puzzle just beyond my grasp, or just a bunch of bullshit dressed up to give a false sense of meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure what's going on but there is that itch that there is an underlying… something to everything happening here, and I can't deny that a lot of the sheer wtfness has me interested. Gonna withhold on voting until things click.

EDIT:

Okay, I think I may have figured it out.
Spoilers? Don't really know.

This is a play on redactions and how information can be obfuscated by them. The Foundation isn't redacting any information here (beyond the FM band); the location is literally named "[DATA REDACTED]" and so is the news channel. The reason behind all the nonsensical object manifestations and the equally bizarre SCP-4329 broadcasts is because these "redactions" are hiding crucial information needed to understand what is happening, and with that information hidden the Foundation can't figure out any rhyme or reason to this.

This is essentially the Foundation dealing with redactions that aren't their own. At least, that's what makes the most sense to me.

If I'm correct then this is very clever, and the fact that the article could keep me interested enough to actually think deeply on it is good, but how obfuscated this was and how it doesn't really go any other directions with the idea is holding back an upvote. I'll need to mull this over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fixed it for them
